compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: A list of all third-party service providers (TPSPs) with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of the cardholder data environment (CDE) is maintained, including a description for each of the services provided.

Written agreements/contracts are maintained with all TPSPs with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of the CDE, including acknowledgement by TPSPs that they are responsible for the security of account data the TPSPs possess or otherwise store, process, or transmit on behalf of the entity, or to the extent that they could impact the security of the entity's CDE.

An established process is implemented for engaging TPSPs, including proper due diligence prior to engagement. Due diligence must assess the TPSP's capability to meet PCI DSS requirements relevant to the services they will provide.

A program is implemented to monitor TPSPs' PCI DSS compliance status at least once every 12 months. The entity must maintain information about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any shared responsibilities.

Information is maintained about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between the TPSP and the entity. A responsibility matrix must be documented and included in or referenced by the contract.

TPSPs provide written acknowledgement to customers that they will maintain all applicable PCI DSS requirements to the extent the TPSP handles, has access to, or otherwise stores, processes, or transmits the customer's account data, or manages the customer's CDE.

TPSPs support their customers' requests for information to meet Requirements 12.8.4 and 12.8.5, providing upon request information about the PCI DSS requirements that are the responsibility of the TPSP and those shared between the TPSP and the customer. This is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it becomes mandatory.

Processes and mechanisms for protecting stored account data are defined and understood. Contracts with TPSPs must specify data retention requirements, disposal methods, and ensure that account data storage is kept to a minimum consistent with business, legal, and regulatory requirements.

Security vulnerabilities are identified and addressed. Contracts with TPSPs that develop or maintain software must require vulnerability identification, risk ranking, and timely patching. Critical and high-security patches must be installed within one month of release.

Media with cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed. Contracts must address physical media handling, secure storage, tracked distribution, and documented destruction procedures when media containing account data is transferred to or handled by TPSPs.

Processes and mechanisms for logging and monitoring all access to system components and cardholder data are defined and documented. Contracts with TPSPs must include requirements for log generation, protection, review, and timely alerting. TPSPs must make logs available for forensic investigation.

External and internal vulnerabilities are regularly identified, prioritised, and addressed. Contracts with TPSPs must include requirements for vulnerability scanning (internal quarterly, external quarterly by ASV, and after significant changes) and annual penetration testing.

An incident response plan is established and maintained to be activated in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident. Contracts must require TPSPs to maintain incident response plans, participate in the entity's incident response process, and provide timely notification of security incidents.

System components are configured and managed securely. Contracts with TPSPs providing system components must require secure configuration standards, removal of unnecessary functionality, and documentation of all enabled services, protocols, and ports with business justification.

A covered entity may disclose protected health information (PHI) to a business associate and may allow a business associate to create, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI on the covered entity's behalf only if the covered entity obtains satisfactory assurances that the business associate will appropriately safeguard the information through a written business associate agreement (BAA).
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: OCR fined Presence Health $475,000 for failure to have a BAA with a business associate (2017).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: A business associate agreement must establish the permitted and required uses and disclosures of PHI by the business associate. The contract may not authorize the business associate to use or further disclose the information in a manner that would violate the HIPAA Privacy Rule if done by the covered entity.

A business associate agreement must provide that the business associate will: (A) not use or further disclose PHI other than as permitted or required by the contract or as required by law; (B) use appropriate safeguards; (C) report to the covered entity any use or disclosure not provided for by the contract; (D) ensure agents/subcontractors agree to the same restrictions; (E) make PHI available to individuals; (F) make PHI available for amendment; (G) make information available for an accounting of disclosures; (H) make internal practices available to HHS; and (I) return or destroy PHI on termination.

The business associate agreement must require the business associate to use appropriate safeguards, and comply with the Security Rule with respect to electronic PHI, to prevent use or disclosure of PHI other than as provided for by the contract.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: OCR fined Advocate Medical Group $5.55 million for inadequate safeguards protecting ePHI (2016).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: A business associate shall, following the discovery of a breach of unsecured PHI, notify the covered entity of such breach without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 calendar days after discovery. The notification shall include: identification of each individual affected, a description of the types of information involved, a description of what the business associate has done to investigate, mitigate, and prevent future breaches.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Premera Blue Cross fined $6.85 million for breach affecting 10.4 million individuals with delayed notification (2020).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: A business associate may disclose PHI to a subcontractor that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits PHI on behalf of the business associate only if the business associate obtains satisfactory assurances, through a written BAA, that the subcontractor will appropriately safeguard the PHI. Flow-down obligations apply to all levels of subcontracting.

A business associate agreement must provide that the covered entity may terminate the contract if the covered entity determines that the business associate has violated a material term of the contract. Upon termination, if feasible, the business associate shall return or destroy all PHI received from, or created or received by the business associate on behalf of, the covered entity.

Upon termination of the contract, if feasible, the business associate shall return or destroy all PHI received from, or created or received by the business associate on behalf of, the covered entity. If return or destruction is not feasible, the protections of the agreement extend to such information and limit further uses and disclosures to those purposes that make return or destruction infeasible.

The business associate agreement must require the business associate to make available PHI in a designated record set to the individual or the covered entity as necessary to satisfy the covered entity's obligations regarding individual access to PHI under 45 CFR 164.524.

The business associate agreement must require the business associate to make available PHI for amendment and incorporate any amendments to PHI in a designated record set as necessary to satisfy the covered entity's obligations under 45 CFR 164.526.
The business associate agreement must require the business associate to make available the information required to provide an accounting of disclosures in accordance with 45 CFR 164.528.

The business associate agreement must require the business associate to make its internal practices, books, and records relating to the use and disclosure of PHI available to the Secretary of HHS for purposes of determining the covered entity's compliance with the HIPAA Rules.

A covered entity is not in compliance with the Security Rule if the covered entity knew of a pattern of activity or practice of the business associate that constituted a material breach or violation of the business associate's obligation under the contract, unless the covered entity took reasonable steps to cure the breach or ended the violation and, if such steps were unsuccessful, terminated the contract.

When using or disclosing PHI or when requesting PHI from another covered entity or business associate, a covered entity or business associate must make reasonable efforts to limit PHI to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose. BAAs must define the minimum necessary PHI for each service.

Management must assess the effectiveness of the issuer's internal control over financial reporting (ICFR). Where financial processes are outsourced to third parties, contracts must ensure the vendor maintains adequate internal controls, provides SOC 1/SOC 2 reports, and cooperates with the entity's external auditors in evaluating control effectiveness.

Criminal penalties for the destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in federal investigations and bankruptcy. Outsourcing contracts involving financial record-keeping must require the vendor to maintain audit trails, prevent record destruction, and retain records for the applicable retention period (minimum 7 years for audit working papers).

Protection for employees of publicly traded companies who provide evidence of fraud. Outsourcing contracts should not include provisions that would prevent or discourage the vendor's employees from reporting potential fraud or violations to regulatory authorities.

Corporate officers must certify the accuracy of financial statements and the effectiveness of internal controls. Where outsourced services affect financial reporting, contracts must ensure the vendor provides sufficient information and access for the officers to make these certifications, including SSAE 18 (SOC 1 Type II) reports.

IT general controls (ITGCs) that are relevant to the entity's internal control over financial reporting must be assessed. Outsourced IT services affecting financial systems must contractually require maintenance of ITGCs covering logical access, change management, computer operations, and program development, with evidence available for the entity's SOX compliance testing.

Ansvar summary: Sensitive authentication data (SAD) is not stored after authorisation, even if encrypted. Contracts with payment processors and TPSPs must expressly prohibit retention of full track data, card verification codes, or PINs/PIN blocks after authorisation completion.

Ansvar summary: Key-management policies and procedures are implemented for cryptographic keys used for protection of stored account data. Contracts with TPSPs handling encryption must specify key management responsibilities, key rotation schedules, split knowledge/dual control, and key custodian obligations.

Ansvar summary: Strong cryptography and security protocols are implemented to safeguard PAN during transmission over open, public networks. Contracts must require TPSPs to use TLS 1.2 or higher for all transmissions containing cardholder data and prohibit use of deprecated protocols.

Ansvar summary: All applicable patches and updates for bespoke and custom software are installed within one month of release for critical/high vulnerabilities. Contracts with software vendors and managed service providers must specify patching SLAs and require evidence of timely patch application.

Ansvar summary: All access into the CDE for administrative access is authenticated via multi-factor authentication. Contracts with TPSPs who access the CDE must require MFA for all remote administrative connections and specify approved MFA methods.

Ansvar summary: A targeted risk analysis is performed for each PCI DSS requirement that provides flexibility for how frequently it is performed. Contracts must require TPSPs to document their targeted risk analyses and make them available during assessments.

Ansvar summary: An incident response plan exists and is ready to be activated in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident involving cardholder data. TPSP contracts must require coordinated incident response, define notification timelines (no later than the entity's PCI DSS obligations), and specify forensic investigation cooperation.

Ansvar summary: Shared hosting providers must protect each entity's hosted environment and cardholder data. Contracts with shared hosting providers must specify logical separation of environments, restriction of access to individual entity environments, and logging/monitoring obligations per hosted entity.

Ansvar summary: Designated entities supplemental validation (DESV) applies to entities designated by a payment brand as requiring additional validation of their PCI DSS compliance. Contracts with designated entities must include enhanced audit rights, quarterly compliance attestation, and immediate notification of compliance status changes.

Ansvar summary: Information is maintained about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between the TPSP and the entity. Contracts must include a responsibility matrix clearly delineating which party manages each applicable PCI DSS requirement.

Ansvar summary: Covered entities and business associates must implement technical policies and procedures for electronic information systems that maintain ePHI to allow access only to persons or software programs that have been granted access rights. BAAs must require the business associate to implement access controls consistent with the covered entity's policies.

Ansvar summary: Assign a unique name and/or number for identifying and tracking user identity. BAAs must require unique user identification for all personnel accessing ePHI and prohibit shared or generic accounts.

Ansvar summary: Implement hardware, software, and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems that contain or use ePHI. BAAs must require the business associate to maintain audit logs of ePHI access and make logs available to the covered entity upon request.

Ansvar summary: Implement policies and procedures to protect ePHI from improper alteration or destruction. BAAs must require the business associate to implement integrity controls and mechanisms to authenticate ePHI.

Ansvar summary: Implement procedures to verify that a person or entity seeking access to ePHI is the one claimed. BAAs must require the business associate to implement authentication mechanisms appropriate to the risk level, including multi-factor authentication for remote access.

Ansvar summary: Implement technical security measures to guard against unauthorised access to ePHI that is being transmitted over an electronic communications network. BAAs must require encryption of ePHI in transit using approved cryptographic standards.

Ansvar summary: Conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of the potential risks and vulnerabilities to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI held by the covered entity or business associate. BAAs must require annual risk assessments and sharing of results relevant to the contracted services.

Ansvar summary: Implement a security awareness and training programme for all members of the workforce. BAAs must require business associates to maintain security awareness training programmes for personnel who handle ePHI and provide evidence of training completion upon request.

Ansvar summary: Implement policies and procedures to address security incidents. BAAs must require the business associate to identify, respond to, mitigate, and document security incidents involving ePHI, and to report incidents to the covered entity within contractually specified timelines.

Ansvar summary: Implement policies and procedures to limit physical access to electronic information systems and the facilities in which they are housed, while ensuring that properly authorised access is allowed. BAAs must require the business associate to implement physical access controls for facilities housing ePHI.
